name: Jomon Pottery Tradition
description: The Jomon people produced some of the world's oldest known pottery, with distinctive cord-marked patterns. These hunter-gatherers developed a remarkably sophisticated culture that lasted over 10,000 years.

contemporarySite.name: Sannai-Maruyama Archaeological Site
contemporarySite.query: Sannai Maruyama Archaeological Site Aomori Japan

name: Introduction of Rice Cultivation
description: Wet-rice paddy farming was introduced to Japan from the Korean Peninsula and mainland Asia, first arriving in northern Kyushu. This agricultural revolution transformed Japanese society from hunter-gatherer to agrarian, enabling population growth and social stratification.

name: Queen Himiko Rules Yamatai
description: According to the Chinese Wei Zhi chronicle, the shamaness-queen Himiko ruled the confederation of Yamatai. She sent envoys to the Wei dynasty in China and received a gold seal, representing Japan's earliest recorded diplomacy.

name: Construction of Nintoku-ryo Kofun
description: The massive keyhole-shaped burial mound attributed to Emperor Nintoku was constructed in Sakai. At 486 meters long, it is one of the largest tombs in the world by area, rivaling the Great Pyramid of Giza.

name: Introduction of Buddhism to Japan
description: The King of Baekje in Korea sent a Buddhist statue and sutras to the Yamato court, traditionally dated to 552. The arrival of Buddhism sparked religious and political conflict but ultimately transformed Japanese culture, art, and governance.

name: Battle of Baekgang
description: Japan sent a massive naval fleet to aid its ally Baekje against the Tang-Silla alliance at the mouth of the Geum River in Korea. The devastating Japanese defeat ended Japan's involvement in Korean peninsula affairs for centuries.

contemporarySite.name: Dazaifu Government Ruins (defensive HQ built after defeat)

name: Taika Reforms
description: After a coup against the powerful Soga clan, Emperor Kotoku enacted sweeping reforms modeled on Tang China's centralized bureaucracy. Land was nationalized, a tax system established, and provincial governance restructured — laying the foundation of the Japanese imperial state.

name: Prince Shotoku's Seventeen-Article Constitution
description: Prince Shotoku promulgated a set of moral guidelines for officials blending Buddhist, Confucian, and Shinto principles. Though not a constitution in the modern sense, it established ideals of harmony and centralized authority that shaped Japanese governance.

name: Jinshin War
description: A succession war between Prince Oama and his nephew Prince Otomo that lasted about a month. Prince Oama's victory led him to become Emperor Tenmu, who strengthened imperial authority and commissioned Japan's first official histories.

contemporarySite.name: Fuwa Barrier Ruins (key defensive point in the war)
contemporarySite.query: Fuwa Barrier Ruins Sekigahara Gifu Japan

name: Kojiki Compiled
description: The Kojiki (Record of Ancient Matters), Japan's oldest surviving written chronicle, was completed. It contains creation myths, genealogies of the gods, and the legendary origins of the imperial line, forming the foundation of Shinto mythology.

name: Capital Moved to Nara (Heijo-kyo)
description: Empress Genmei established the first permanent capital at Nara, modeled on the Chinese Tang capital Chang'an. The city became a center of Buddhist art, scholarship, and international exchange along the eastern terminus of the Silk Road.

name: Great Buddha of Todaiji Completed
description: Emperor Shomu dedicated the Great Buddha (Daibutsu) at Todaiji, a 15-meter bronze statue representing Vairocana Buddha. The project consumed most of Japan's bronze production and symbolized the state's embrace of Buddhism as a unifying force.

name: Capital Moved to Heian-kyo (Kyoto)
description: Emperor Kanmu relocated the capital to Heian-kyo to escape the political influence of Nara's powerful Buddhist monasteries. Kyoto would remain the imperial capital for over a thousand years, becoming synonymous with Japanese high culture.

name: Tale of Genji Written
description: Lady Murasaki Shikibu completed The Tale of Genji, widely considered the world's first novel. The work depicts the romantic and political life of the Heian court with extraordinary psychological depth, and it remains a masterpiece of world literature.

contemporarySite.name: The Tale of Genji Museum
contemporarySite.query: Tale of Genji Museum Uji Kyoto Japan

name: Fujiwara Clan Dominance (Regency Government)
description: Fujiwara no Michinaga reached the height of his power, marrying his daughters to successive emperors and ruling as regent. His era epitomized the Fujiwara domination of the court, which lasted for centuries through strategic marriage politics.

name: Former Nine Years' War
description: Minamoto no Yoriyoshi waged a prolonged campaign against the Abe clan in northern Honshu. The war established the Minamoto as a major warrior clan and demonstrated the growing military power of provincial samurai families.

name: Hogen Rebellion
description: A brief but violent power struggle between rival claimants to the throne drew the Taira and Minamoto warrior clans directly into court politics for the first time. The conflict marked the beginning of the end of civilian aristocratic rule.

name: Heiji Rebellion
description: Minamoto no Yoshitomo launched a coup against the Taira-backed court and was crushed by Taira no Kiyomori. The Taira consolidated control and Kiyomori's young grandson was placed on the throne, setting the stage for the Genpei War.

name: Mount Koya Monastery Founded
description: The monk Kukai (Kobo Daishi) founded a monastery on Mount Koya as the headquarters of Shingon Buddhism. It became one of Japan's most sacred sites, housing over 100 temples and a vast cemetery where Kukai is believed to rest in eternal meditation.

contemporarySite.name: Okunoin Cemetery, Mount Koya
contemporarySite.query: Okunoin Cemetery Mount Koya Wakayama Japan

name: Genpei War Begins
description: Minamoto no Yoritomo raised an army against the Taira clan, beginning the five-year Genpei War. The conflict engulfed western Japan and culminated in the destruction of the Taira, fundamentally transforming Japan's political structure.

name: Battle of Dan-no-ura
description: The decisive naval battle that ended the Genpei War, resulting in the defeat of the Taira clan by the Minamoto. The child emperor Antoku perished, and the Imperial Regalia were lost to the sea.

name: Kamakura Shogunate Founded
description: Minamoto no Yoritomo was appointed Seii Taishogun, establishing Japan's first military government in Kamakura. This marked the beginning of nearly 700 years of samurai rule.

name: First Mongol Invasion
description: Kublai Khan launched his first invasion of Japan with 900 ships and 40,000 troops landing at Hakata Bay. A sudden storm destroyed much of the Mongol fleet, forcing a retreat.

contemporarySite.name: Genko Borui (Mongol Invasion Stone Wall)
contemporarySite.query: Genko Borui Mongol Invasion Wall Fukuoka Japan

name: Second Mongol Invasion
description: Kublai Khan's second and far larger invasion force of 140,000 men again targeted Hakata Bay. A massive typhoon — the divine wind, or kamikaze — obliterated the fleet and ended the Mongol threat.

name: Onin War Begins
description: A dispute over shogunal succession ignited the Onin War in Kyoto, which lasted a decade and left the capital in ruins. It ushered in the Sengoku period of a century of civil war.

contemporarySite.name: Nishiki Tenmangu Shrine (near Onin War site)

name: Battle of Kawanakajima
description: The fourth and most famous of five battles between Takeda Shingen and Uesugi Kenshin on the plains of Kawanakajima in Shinano Province. The battle ended in a bloody stalemate.

contemporarySite.name: Kawanakajima Battlefield Historic Park
contemporarySite.query: Kawanakajima Battlefield Historic Park Nagano Japan

name: Battle of Nagashino
description: Oda Nobunaga and Tokugawa Ieyasu decimated the Takeda cavalry using coordinated volley fire from 3,000 arquebusiers, revolutionizing Japanese warfare and demonstrating the dominance of firearms.

contemporarySite.name: Nagashino Castle Ruins & Battle Site
contemporarySite.query: Nagashino Castle Ruins Shinshiro Aichi Japan

name: Honnoji Incident
description: Oda Nobunaga was betrayed and surrounded by the forces of his general Akechi Mitsuhide at Honnoji temple in Kyoto. Nobunaga, on the verge of unifying Japan, died there — either killed or by suicide.

name: Battle of Sekigahara
description: The largest and most decisive battle in Japanese history, fought between Tokugawa Ieyasu's eastern coalition and Ishida Mitsunari's western coalition. Tokugawa's victory paved the way for the Edo Shogunate.

contemporarySite.name: Sekigahara Battlefield Memorial Museum
contemporarySite.query: Sekigahara Battlefield Memorial Museum Gifu Japan

name: Siege of Osaka
description: Tokugawa Ieyasu besieged Osaka Castle, the stronghold of Toyotomi Hideyori. The fall of the castle and death of Hideyori eliminated the last major threat to Tokugawa rule and unified Japan under the shogunate.

name: Edo Shogunate Established
description: Tokugawa Ieyasu was appointed Seii Taishogun and established his government in Edo. The Tokugawa shogunate would rule Japan for over 260 years, ushering in a long era of peace and isolation.

contemporarySite.name: Edo Castle (Imperial Palace East Gardens)
contemporarySite.query: Imperial Palace East Gardens Tokyo Japan

name: Shimabara Rebellion
description: A peasant and Christian uprising in the Shimabara Peninsula led by the young Amakusa Shiro. The shogunate crushed the revolt with overwhelming force, strengthening the ban on Christianity and closing Japan to the West.

name: Dejima Trading Post Opens
description: The Dutch East India Company was confined to the artificial island of Dejima in Nagasaki harbor, becoming the only Westerners permitted to trade with Japan during sakoku (the closed country period).

name: Great Fire of Meireki
description: A catastrophic fire swept through Edo over three days, destroying about two-thirds of the city and killing an estimated 100,000 people. It was the deadliest urban fire in Japanese history and led to major rebuilding of the capital.

name: 47 Ronin Incident
description: Forty-seven samurai, made masterless after their lord Asano Naganori was forced to commit seppuku, avenged his death by killing the official Kira Yoshinaka. They were ordered to commit ritual suicide but became celebrated as paragons of loyalty.

name: Hoei Eruption of Mt. Fuji
description: The last eruption of Mount Fuji, occurring just 49 days after the great Hoei earthquake. Ash fell on Edo (now Tokyo) 100 km away, devastating crops and causing widespread hardship across the Kanto region.

name: Great Kyoho Famine
description: A devastating famine struck western Japan caused by locust infestations and flooding, killing an estimated one million people. The disaster exposed weaknesses in the Tokugawa system and triggered rice riots in Edo.

name: Perry's Black Ships Arrive
description: U.S. Commodore Matthew Perry arrived at Uraga harbor with four steam-powered warships, demanding Japan open to trade. The technological disparity shocked Japan and set off political turmoil that led to the Meiji Restoration.

contemporarySite.name: Perry Road & Black Ship Memorial, Shimoda

name: Opening of Yokohama Port
description: Yokohama was opened as a treaty port under the Harris Treaty, becoming the primary gateway for foreign trade and culture. The port transformed Japan's economy and accelerated Western influence before the Meiji Restoration.

contemporarySite.name: Yokohama Port Opening Memorial Hall
contemporarySite.query: Yokohama Port Opening Memorial Hall Japan

name: Sakuradamon Incident
description: Ii Naosuke, the powerful regent who signed the unequal treaties opening Japan, was assassinated outside Edo Castle's Sakuradamon gate by anti-shogunate samurai. The killing shattered the image of shogunal invincibility.

contemporarySite.name: Sakuradamon Gate, Imperial Palace
contemporarySite.query: Sakuradamon Gate Imperial Palace Tokyo Japan

name: Meiji Restoration
description: The Tokugawa shogunate was overthrown and imperial rule was restored under Emperor Meiji. The new government launched a sweeping program of modernization, industrialization, and Westernization that transformed Japan from a feudal society into a modern nation-state.

name: Boshin War — Battle of Toba-Fushimi
description: The opening battle of the Boshin War between pro-imperial Satsuma-Choshu forces and the Tokugawa shogunate army. The imperial victory near Kyoto triggered the rapid collapse of shogunal resistance across Japan.

contemporarySite.name: Toba-Fushimi Battle Monument
contemporarySite.query: Toba Fushimi Battle Monument Kyoto Japan

name: First Sino-Japanese War
description: Japan fought and defeated Qing China over influence in Korea, winning control of Taiwan and the Liaodong Peninsula. The stunning victory demonstrated Japan's successful modernization and announced it as a major power in East Asia.

contemporarySite.name: Shimonoseki City (Treaty of Shimonoseki signed here)

name: Russo-Japanese War
description: Japan waged war against Tsarist Russia over territorial disputes in Manchuria and Korea. Japan's naval victory at Tsushima and the Treaty of Portsmouth made it the first modern Asian nation to defeat a European power.

name: Great Kanto Earthquake
description: A magnitude 7.9 earthquake struck the Kanto plain, devastating Tokyo and Yokohama. Fires raged for days, killing over 100,000 people and leaving millions homeless. The disaster reshaped Tokyo's urban planning and architecture.

contemporarySite.name: Great Kanto Earthquake Memorial Museum
contemporarySite.query: Tokyo Memorial Hall Great Kanto Earthquake Sumida Japan

name: Meiji Constitution Promulgated
description: Emperor Meiji granted the Constitution of the Empire of Japan, establishing Asia's first parliamentary system. It created a bicameral legislature (the Diet) while preserving strong imperial prerogatives and executive power.

name: Manchurian Incident
description: Japanese military officers staged a false-flag bombing on the South Manchuria Railway near Mukden, using it as a pretext to invade and occupy Manchuria. The incident marked the ascendancy of the military over civilian government and Japan's path toward wider war.

name: Rice Riots
description: Soaring rice prices triggered massive protests and riots across Japan, beginning in Toyama Prefecture. Over two million people participated, leading to the fall of the government and accelerating the movement toward universal suffrage and party politics.

contemporarySite.name: Rice Riot Memorial, Uozu
contemporarySite.query: Rice Riot Memorial Uozu Toyama Japan

name: Universal Male Suffrage Act
description: Japan enacted universal male suffrage, granting the vote to all men over 25 regardless of wealth or tax status. This quadrupled the electorate, though it was accompanied by the Peace Preservation Act that restricted political dissent.

name: February 26 Incident
description: Young ultranationalist army officers launched a coup in Tokyo, assassinating several senior government officials and occupying key buildings. Though the coup failed, it accelerated the military's dominance over Japanese politics in the lead-up to World War II.

contemporarySite.name: February 26 Incident Memorial
contemporarySite.query: February 26 Incident Memorial Roppongi Tokyo Japan

name: Atomic Bombing of Hiroshima
description: The United States dropped the first atomic bomb used in warfare on Hiroshima, instantly killing an estimated 80,000 people with tens of thousands more dying from radiation in the following months. The bombing hastened Japan's surrender and the end of World War II.

contemporarySite.name: Hiroshima Peace Memorial (Genbaku Dome)
contemporarySite.query: Hiroshima Peace Memorial Genbaku Dome Japan

name: Atomic Bombing of Nagasaki
description: Three days after Hiroshima, a second atomic bomb was dropped on Nagasaki, killing approximately 40,000 people instantly. Emperor Hirohito announced Japan's surrender the following day, ending the Pacific War.

name: New Constitution Takes Effect
description: Japan's postwar constitution came into effect, renouncing war (Article 9), establishing popular sovereignty, and guaranteeing fundamental human rights including women's suffrage. Drafted during the American occupation, it has never been amended.

name: Treaty of San Francisco
description: Japan signed a peace treaty with 48 nations, formally ending World War II and restoring Japanese sovereignty. The treaty came into effect in 1952, ending the Allied occupation, though the U.S. retained military bases under a separate security treaty.

name: Tokyo Olympics
description: Tokyo hosted the Summer Olympic Games, the first Olympics held in Asia. The event showcased Japan's dramatic postwar recovery and technological prowess, highlighted by the debut of the Shinkansen bullet train just days before the opening ceremony.

name: Shinkansen (Bullet Train) Inaugurated
description: The Tokaido Shinkansen between Tokyo and Osaka began service, becoming the world's first high-speed rail line. Traveling at 210 km/h, it revolutionized transportation and became an enduring symbol of Japan's technological innovation.

name: Okinawa Reversion
description: The United States returned administrative control of Okinawa to Japan after 27 years of American military governance. The reversion fulfilled a long-standing Japanese demand, though large U.S. military bases remained on the island.

contemporarySite.name: Okinawa Prefectural Peace Memorial Museum
contemporarySite.query: Okinawa Prefectural Peace Memorial Museum Japan

name: Great Hanshin Earthquake
description: A magnitude 6.9 earthquake struck Kobe and surrounding areas, killing over 6,400 people and causing $100 billion in damage. The disaster exposed inadequacies in earthquake preparedness and led to major reforms in building codes and disaster response.

contemporarySite.name: Kobe Earthquake Memorial Museum
contemporarySite.query: Disaster Reduction and Human Renovation Institution Kobe Japan

name: Tohoku Earthquake and Tsunami
description: A magnitude 9.0 earthquake off the Pacific coast triggered a massive tsunami that devastated northeastern Japan, killing over 18,000 people. The tsunami also caused the Fukushima Daiichi nuclear meltdown, the worst nuclear disaster since Chernobyl.

name: Emperor Akihito Abdicates
description: Emperor Akihito became the first Japanese emperor to abdicate in over 200 years, citing age and health. His son Naruhito ascended to the Chrysanthemum Throne, beginning the Reiwa era — a moment of national reflection and renewal.
